docs.title: Welcome to My EECS Portfolio
docs.text: Welcome! This website serves as my public digital brain. It documents my progress as I self-teach Computer Science, Electrical Engineering, Math, & Physics while also hosting my personal tech blogs, vlogs(maybe), research notes, and a directory of my learning resources.

docs.title: 📚 Current Courses & Full Curriculums Compiled by ME
docs.text:
These are the university courses I am currently working through:

  • Computer Science — Curriculum
    • MIT 6.100L: Intro to CS and Programming using Python

docs.title: 🔬 Research & Projects
docs.text: Beyond coursework, I actively explore specific tech domains and participate in independent research. * (Future UROP and project links will be indexed here)

docs.title: ✍️ Tech Blog & Deep Dives
docs.text:
I don't just write code; I write about tech. Here are my latest breakdowns of current events and industry concepts:

  • (Future Tech Blog links will be indexed here)

docs.title: 🌍 Community & Influences (Referenced Sites)
docs.text:
I stand on the shoulders of giants. These are the open-source curriculums and fellow self-taught developers who inspire this site:

  • OSSU (Open Source Society University): The gold standard for a free, self-taught CS curriculum.

  • CS DIY Wiki (PKUFlyingPig): A brilliant comprehensive guide to self-learning CS.

  • Coding Interview University: A multi-month study plan for becoming a software engineer for a large company.

  • Nightmare: An incredible intro course and resource for reverse engineering and binary exploitation.

  • Artoriuz OSEE: An excellent and comprehensive 3 year curriculum to self-learn Electrical Engineering

  • Awesome Electronics: Amazing list of resources for learning Electronics.

  • Computer Science Lectures: A great online bookmarks/lookup table for freely available online video courses/lectures.

  • Reverse Engineering & Malware Analysis Roadmap: An amazing and comprehensive roadmap for mastering reverse engineering and malware analysis.

  • Awesome LLM Security: A curation of awesome tools, documents and projects about LLM Security.

  • InfoSec Reference: My favorite free resource for anyone interested in learning more about Information Security.

  • NYU Tandon's OSIRIS Lab's Hack Night: Uumm! My 2nd favorite security resource.

  • Awesome Reversing: A curated list of awesome reversing resources

  • Awesome Computer Science: A curated lists of resources for various fields in Computer Science.

  • Cybersecurity Resources: A collection of links, notes, programs, websites, etc. related to CTFs and cybersecurity.

docs.title: 🛠️ Resource Libraries
docs.text:
Where I source my textbooks, documentation, lectures, and research papers:

  • Z-Library (Digital library for textbooks)

  • Anna's Archive (Open-source search engine for papers and books)

  • arXiv.org (Cornell's free archive for open-access research papers)

  • Learn Anything (A free, comprehensive platform that connects you to the best resources for every subject.)

  • Build your own X (This repository is a compilation of well-written, step-by-step guides for re-creating our favorite technologies from scratch.)

  • OpenSecurity Training2: A gold mine for Reverse engineering. My 3rd favorite. lol!

docs.title: 🏛️ Academic Institutions & Platforms
docs.text:
I am incredibly grateful to the world-class universities and digital platforms that make their high-level curriculums available to the public. The coursework, lectures, and resources documented on this site are heavily sourced from:

  • Universities:

    • MIT

    • Stanford University

    • Yale University

    • UC Berkeley

    • Harvard University

    • ETH Zurich

    • Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania

    • Carnegie Mellon University

  • Learning Platforms:

    • Coursera

    • edX

    • MITx

docs.title: MIT 6.100L - Fall 2022: Introduction to Computer Science and Programming Using Python
Description
docs.text: This subject is aimed at students with little to no programming experience. It aims to provide students with an understanding of the role computation can play in solving problems. It also aims to help students, regardless of their major, feel justifiably confident in their ability to write simple programs that allow them to accomplish useful goals. The class will use the Python 3 programming language This class is a full-semester version of 6.100A (formerly 6.0001 Introduction to Computer Science and Programming in Python).

docs.title: Readings
docs.text: Textbook: John Guttag’s Introduction to Computation and Programming Using Python, Third Edition, with Application to Computational Modeling and Understanding Data

docs.title: 📂 Course Materials
docs.text:
Welcome to my active workspace for this class. Below you will find my compiled notes, practice code, and specific research related to the 6.100L curriculum:

  • 📝 Lecture Notes & Concepts

  • 💻 Code Snippets & Syntax Practice

  • 🚀 Problem Sets

  • 🚀 Projects

  • 🔬 Course-Specific Research (Note: Core findings are also cross-referenced in my main Research tab).

docs.title: 📅 Course Calendar
docs.text:
In-person lectures were mandatory and occurred on Mondays and Wednesdays. Recitations were held on Fridays and were optional.

Finger Ex. stands for "Finger Exercises" and "PS" stands for "Problem Set."

docs.title: Grading Policy
docs.text:
Problem sets: 45%

Completion of mandatory finger exercises: 10%

Microquizzes (best 7 out of 10): 45%
